content:
That's an interesting question! To address it carefully, let's look at the evidence about Time magazine's Person of the Year selections in relation to U.S. presidents.

Time's Person of the Year is an annual issue of the magazine that features a person, group, idea, or object that "for better or for worse... has done the most to influence the events of the year." Historically, many U.S. presidents have been selected for this honor.

To verify whether every U.S. president has been selected at least once, we can examine documented lists of Time's Person of the Year recipients:

- Presidents like Franklin D. Roosevelt, John F. Kennedy, Richard Nixon, Ronald Reagan, Bill Clinton, George W. Bush, Barack Obama, and Donald Trump have all been named Person of the Year in some year of their presidency or other influence.

- Some presidents who served before Time magazine's founding in 1923 obviously were never selected. For presidents after 1923, the question is whether any have been skipped.

- Checking the list, for example, Jimmy Carter was Time's Person of the Year in 1976 as "Man of the Year." Similarly, Gerald Ford was selected in 1974.

- The most recent presidents—Joe Biden, for instance—have not yet been named Person of the Year (as of mid-2024). Joe Biden has not been selected as Person of the Year while in office. So, if the question concerns the most recent president who was not selected, that would be Joe Biden.

Therefore, the claim that "All presidents have been selected as Time's Person of the Year" is not supported by the evidence as of now, because Joe Biden has not received that designation.

In summary:

- Many U.S. presidents have been named Person of the Year.

- Some presidents, including Joe Biden, have not been named Person of the Year.

- The reason many presidents have been selected is that the role of U.S. president is highly
